title: The Great Train Robbery: Undone by a Monopoly Board
description: In August 1963, fifteen men stopped the Glasgow to London mail train at a rigged red signal in the Buckinghamshire countryside and unloaded 2.6 million pounds in used banknotes in under half an hour. No guns fired, no alarm raised, the biggest cash robbery Britain had ever seen. Then they holed up in a farmhouse to wait out the manhunt, and somebody dealt out a game of Monopoly.

sources.text: Piers Paul Read, 'The Train Robbers,' W.H. Allen, 1978
Bruce Reynolds, 'The Autobiography of a Thief,' Bantam Press, 1995
Nick Russell-Pavier and Stewart Richards, 'The Great Train Robbery: Crime of the Century,' Weidenfeld & Nicolson, 2013
The National Archives (UK), case files on the 1963 Cheddington mail train robbery, DPP 2/3715 series and HO 287/1496
BBC News coverage of the robbery's fiftieth anniversary and the December 2013 obituary of Ronnie Biggs

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Okay, completely unrelated to anything, I took a train this weekend, three hours down the coast, and I spent basically the whole ride staring at other people's luggage thinking, what would Frankie take.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: That's a normal thing our show has done to you.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: There was a man with a locked aluminum briefcase, Frankie. Chained to the rack. I almost asked him what was in it.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Never ask. The answer is always insurance paperwork, and it ruins the mystery.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: This is why we're friends. Okay. What did you bring me? And before you answer, I want it on the record that you promised me the Eiffel Tower. Last time, you stood there and said, next time, a man who sold the Eiffel Tower for scrap. Twice. I have been thinking about him for two weeks.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: He's coming, and he's worth the wait. But while I was reading up on him, I fell down a hole. And at the bottom of the hole was a Monopoly board.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: A Monopoly board.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: A Monopoly board that helped put some of the most wanted men in England behind bars for thirty years apiece.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: I'm sorry, the board game? Top hat, little dog, do not pass go?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Especially that last part. England, August 1963. In the middle of the night, in the middle of nowhere, a gang stops a Royal Mail train and walks away with two point six million pounds in used banknotes. No guns fired. No alarm raised. The biggest cash robbery the country had ever seen. The papers called it the crime of the century.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: And the Monopoly board comes in where?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: The gang drives the money to a little farmhouse to wait out the manhunt. And to pass the time while the entire English police force hunts them, they play Monopoly.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Please tell me they played with the real money.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: They played with the real money.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: [laughs] Obviously. You're sitting on a hundred and twenty mailbags of cash, you are not going to use the pastel paper that comes in the box.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: And every man who took his turn at that board left a little something behind on it.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: No. Fingerprints. The Monopoly board testified.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: In a manner of speaking, it did.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Okay, yeah, I'm in. I'm Ruby, she's Frankie, and this is Two Grifters.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Today: the Great Train Robbery. Fifteen men, one mail train, two point six million pounds, and the family board game that helped undo the crime of the century.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Bank error in your favor. Collect thirty years.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Before anybody steals anything, I need to explain the train, because the train is wonderful. It was called a Travelling Post Office. Picture an ordinary night train, except most of it is a working post office on wheels. Sorting frames, pigeonholes, racks of mailbags, and a crew of sorters standing at their stations sorting letters at fifty miles an hour, all night, every night.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Wait, they sorted the mail on the train? While it moved?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: That was the whole point. A letter posted in Scotland in the evening came off the train in London already sorted, ready for the morning rounds. This particular service left Glasgow at ten to seven each evening and pulled into London Euston around dawn. Twelve coaches. The staff called it the Up Special.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: The Up Special. Very cute. And where does the money come in?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Second coach from the engine. The H V P coach. High Value Packages. Here's the part I need you to sit with. Every day, banks in Scotland and the north of England mailed their surplus cash down to their London head offices. Old notes, worn notes, notes on their way out of circulation to be destroyed. They packed the money into ordinary white mailbags and sent it south on the mail train.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Hold on. Hold on. The banks mailed the cash? Like, posted it?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Registered mail. It had worked for a century, so nobody thought about it anymore.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Okay, but I know this one, this is the gold train. This is the one where they got the gold bars, right?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: No gold. Better than gold. Used five pound notes. One pound notes. Ten shilling notes. Money that had already been through a thousand hands, headed for the furnace. Unmarked, unlisted, untraceable.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: So it's the one kind of money in the world nobody can follow, and it's riding in a mail sack.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: On a scheduled service. Same route, same coach position, every night of the week. On a normal night that one coach carried something like three hundred thousand pounds.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: A rolling bank vault with a printed timetable.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: And then fortune leans all the way in. August bank holiday. Long weekend, the whole country spends money, and every bank in Scotland ends up sitting on extra cash. All of it goes south on the next few trains. On the night of the seventh of August, 1963, the Up Special was carrying a hundred and twenty eight bags of it. Around two point six million pounds.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: What is that in modern money? Ballpark.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Comfortably north of fifty million.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Fifty million. On the overnight mail. Guarded by, what? Please say guards.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Postal workers. Some seventy sorters spread through the train, doing their jobs. No armed guard. No police aboard. There'd never been a reason.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: You're joking.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Oh, it gets better. The Post Office did own special high value coaches. Reinforced, barred windows, alarms wired in. Three of them existed. And that week, all three were out of service at once. So the money rode in the reserve coach. Ordinary body, ordinary locks, no alarm.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: All three. The same week. Is that a coincidence, or did somebody arrange the world's most convenient maintenance schedule?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: The record says breakdowns and repairs, and nobody has ever proven otherwise. But it's a fair question, because this gang knew things they had no business knowing. Which trains carried the big loads. Which coach. How many bags. And exactly when the bank holiday surge would come down the line. All of it came from an inside man.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: A postman?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: To this day, nobody can say for certain who he was. The gang themselves only ever knew him by a nickname: the Ulsterman. He met them on park benches, took his cut in cash, and walked out of history. Hold that thought, because we will come back to him at the very end.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: An anonymous man on a park bench who points at a train and says, that one.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: That one, on that night. And the men he pointed it out to were, unfortunately for the Post Office, the best thieves in London.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Our ringleader is Bruce Reynolds. Antiques dealer by cover story, thief by profession. He drives an Aston Martin, wears tailored suits, holidays on the Riviera, and plans robberies the way a staff officer plans a campaign. His friends called him Napoleon.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Of course they did.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: And Reynolds has a problem. A train is not a bank. You can't tunnel into it, you can't blow the doors off it, and it refuses to stand still. To rob the Up Special he has to do something almost unheard of in his world. He has to merge two rival firms into one crew.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: A merger. Do criminals do due diligence?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: More carefully than most companies. Firm one is Reynolds' own South West London crowd. Gordon Goody, big, meticulous, maybe the best pure thief in the country. Buster Edwards, charm and muscle. Charlie Wilson, the treasurer, so unflappable the press would later call him the silent man. And Roy James, a racing driver. Genuinely fast, people who saw him race said he could have gone professional. His job is the getaway.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: And firm two?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Firm two is a crew the police called the South Coast Raiders, and their specialty, Ruby, is trains. They had been quietly robbing them for years. And their star is a soft-spoken florist named Roger Cordrey.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: A florist.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: A florist who understood railway signalling the way you and I understand a light switch. Cordrey had worked out how to stop any train in England exactly where he wanted it, using things you could buy in a hardware shop.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Wait, but that's the whole heist, then. If you can stop the train wherever you want, you've already won.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: You've won for about half an hour. After that, seventy postal workers, the railway, and every police force in the country notice that the mail is missing. The genius was never stopping the train. The genius is what you do with the half hour.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Okay. So how big does this get? How many names am I keeping track of?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Around fifteen men at the track on the night. Sixteen or seventeen in the plan, depending on who's counting. And accounts genuinely differ on that number, for a very good reason: some of them were never caught, never named, never identified. There are men in this story with no faces.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: That's going to bother me all episode.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: It bothered the police for decades. And one more name, the one you already know. Ronnie Biggs. Small-time. In and out of prison, trying to go straight as a builder, and short about five hundred pounds for his house. He came to Reynolds, an old friend, to borrow money. Reynolds offered him something better, a seat on the job, because Biggs claimed to have the one thing the firm couldn't buy.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Which was?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: A train driver. Biggs knew a retired driver, a man the gang only ever called Pop. The plan needed someone to move the train after they stopped it, and nobody fancied learning on the night. So Ronnie Biggs's entire contribution to the crime of the century amounts to: I know a guy.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Hm. A retired train driver. Retired how long, exactly? Retired from driving what?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Hold that thought too.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: [laughs] That's two thoughts I'm holding, Frankie. I have small hands.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Last piece of the machine. The hideout. Most robbers grab the bag and scatter to the winds. Reynolds decides his crew will do the opposite. They'll vanish into the countryside, sit tight, and let the roadblocks get bored looking for them. So through a bent solicitor's clerk named Brian Field, the gang goes shopping. And it buys a farm.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Buys? You do not buy the getaway house! Buying is paperwork! It's the least anonymous thing a human being can do!

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Leatherslade Farm. A tired little farmhouse with sheds and a garage, up its own private track, no neighbors in sight, twenty seven miles from the stretch of railway they'd chosen. Bought through Field's law firm in another man's name. Deposit paid, keys handed over, and the gang moved in like the world's least convincing farmers.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Did they farm even slightly?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: They painted a lorry army green, and they stocked the kitchen for a siege. Tinned soup, baked beans, tea, beer, eggs. And in among the supplies, somebody packed a Monopoly set.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Chekhov's board game, on the kitchen table.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Loaded, and waiting for act three.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: The night of the seventh of August, 1963. The gang rolls out of the farm in convoy. The army lorry and two Land Rovers, fifteen men in army fatigues and balaclavas, because if anyone spots them on the lanes at midnight, they want to read as a military exercise.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: A midnight army convoy being, somehow, the least suspicious available option.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: In 1963, with bases all over the county, it genuinely was. They're heading for a lonely stretch of line in Buckinghamshire, near a village called Ledburn. Two places on the map matter. A signal gantry called Sears Crossing. And half a mile further down the line, a low bridge over a country lane, Bridego Bridge. Stop the train at the signal. Rob it at the bridge, where the lorry waits in the lane below. That's the entire shape of the night.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: And Cordrey works his flower magic where?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: At the signals, and it comes in two parts. Further up the line there's an amber warning signal. Cordrey's men rig it to glow amber, so the driver eases off, expecting trouble ahead. Then at Sears Crossing itself, the red. They cover the green lamp with a glove, an ordinary workman's glove. And they clip a battery to the red lamp to light it up.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: A glove and a battery. That's the hack? Fifty million pounds, stopped with the contents of a kitchen drawer?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Because a driver cannot pass a red signal. Not will not. Cannot. Every rule, every instinct, every year of training says a red light means another train stopped dead ahead in the dark. The signal doesn't have to be real. It has to be red.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: They didn't hack the railway at all. They hacked the driver.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Three in the morning, August the eighth. The Up Special comes down the line, catches the amber, slows, sees the red at Sears Crossing, and sighs to a stop. Up in the cab are two men. The driver, Jack Mills, fifty seven years old, from Crewe, a lifetime on the railways. Beside him his fireman, David Whitby, twenty six.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: And seventy sorters behind them with no idea anything is wrong.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: A signal stop is the most normal thing in the world. Whitby climbs down to ring the signalman from the lineside phone, standard procedure, and finds the wires have been cut. He turns, sees a man standing between the coaches, walks over to him. And the man takes him by the arm, and very quietly promises to kill him if he shouts. Whitby goes down the embankment with a hand over his mouth.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Oh, I hate this part.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: The cab fills with men in balaclavas. And Jack Mills does the thing you are not supposed to do. He fights. Fifty seven years old, and he gets one of them halfway back out of the cab door. Then someone behind him swings a cosh, and it comes down on his head.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: God.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: He goes down bleeding badly. And I want to stop on this, because everything else about tonight is clever and bloodless, and this is neither. No one was ever charged with striking Jack Mills. The gang spent the rest of their lives pointing at each other. One of them confessed to it on his deathbed, half a century later, and many of the people who know this case best don't believe him. Accounts differ, and they differ to this day.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: The one moment in the whole night that nobody wants to own.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: The one moment. Now the clever part collides with the stupid part. The plan says uncouple the engine and the first two coaches, the cash coach and the one ahead of it, and drive just that half mile down to the bridge. The other ten coaches, and almost all seventy sorters, get left sitting peacefully at a red light in the dark, sorting the mail of a train that is quietly being stolen out from under them.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Which is why they need a driver of their own. Pop! It's Pop's big moment!

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: They lift Pop up into the cab of a mainline diesel locomotive. And Pop cannot make it move.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: What do you mean, he cannot make it move?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: He'd spent his whole career on little shunting engines in a rail yard. He had never handled anything like this machine. He can't get the brake pressure up, the locomotive will not budge, and the whole timetable starts bleeding out while an old man pats at the controls of an unfamiliar engine like it's a nervous horse.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: They stole a train. And brought a driver who could not drive the train.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: So they pull Pop back out of the seat, and they haul Jack Mills up off the floor, blood running into his eyes, and they tell him: you drive. And Mills, concussed, surrounded, does it. He drives his own train half a mile down the line to a white sheet the gang has pegged up beside the track at Bridego Bridge.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: They made the man they had just beaten do the job their own guy couldn't. I need a second with that one.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: At the bridge, it turns into pure muscle. They break into the cash coach, put the handful of sorters inside face down on the floor, and form a human chain down the embankment to the lorry in the lane. A hundred and twenty mailbags go down the bank, hand to hand to hand. Two and a half tons of paper money, in well under half an hour.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Out of a hundred and twenty eight. They left bags on the train?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Eight bags, sitting right there in the coach. Reynolds ran the raid on a stopwatch, and when it hit the limit, a voice down the chain said: that's it, time's up. And they stopped. Mid-fortune. Walked away from the rest.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: That might be the most disciplined thing I've ever heard. Leaving sacks of money on the floor.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: That discipline is why they got away clean. One last instruction before the convoy pulls out. They lean into the coach and tell the postal men: nobody moves for half an hour. Then it's engines low, back lanes, headlights off under the moon, and by sunrise the whole two point six million is stacked on the kitchen floor of a farmhouse, and the kettle is on.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: England's biggest robbery ends with a cup of tea.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Most things there do.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: The count takes most of a day. Bag by bag, bundle by bundle, stacked around the living room in walls of paper. The final police figure, from the bank manifests, was two million, five hundred and ninety five thousand, nine hundred and ninety seven pounds.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: To the pound. I'm sorry, hearing it counted out like that is different. That's not a robbery, that's a payroll.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Biggest in British history at that point, by a distance. A full share came out at something like a hundred and fifty thousand pounds a man, though accounts differ on the exact splits. There were side payments, drinks for the helpers, a cut set aside for the Ulsterman. For scale, a decent house in England that year cost about three thousand pounds. Every man in that farmhouse is holding a whole street.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: And the mood is what? Champagne? Cigars?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Tea and the wireless. They've got a radio tuned to the police frequencies, they're cooking for fifteen on a rota, washing up in shifts. The plan is to sit tight for a week or more, until the roadblocks come down and the county gets bored. And in the evenings, to pass the time, out comes the Monopoly board.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: With the real money. Say it again for the people in the back.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Rent collected in genuine used five pound notes. Fresh from the mail train.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: [laughs] Landing on Mayfair and paying with evidence. Who won?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: History does not record who won.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Cowards. Okay. When does it go wrong? Because I can feel it going wrong.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: It goes wrong on the radio, and it goes wrong because of politeness. That last instruction at the bridge. Nobody moves for half an hour.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: What's wrong with... oh. Oh no.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Say it.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Half an hour means they only needed half an hour. It means the hideout is half an hour away. They handed the police a radius.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: A detective told the press almost exactly that. The men they wanted, he said, would be found within thirty miles of the track. And then the police announced it to the country: every farm, every barn, every empty building inside a thirty mile circle would be visited and searched.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: And Leatherslade Farm sits at mile...?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Twenty seven. The gang heard their own doom announced over the radio, in the hideout they had chosen because it was so beautifully tucked away, twenty seven miles from the bridge. Just inside the circle the police had drawn around them.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: So they run.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: They scatter days ahead of schedule. Wipe-downs, gloves, a bonfire of kit in the garden. And the plan had one last safety net. An associate had been paid, and paid well, to come back after they'd gone, scrub the farm to the studs, and burn it to the ground.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Let me guess. He took the money and never went.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Never so much as drove past the gate. So on the thirteenth of August, five days after the robbery, a herdsman who'd been wondering about the odd comings and goings up that track finally mentions the little farm to the police. Officers walk in and find a freshly painted lorry, two Land Rovers with matching number plates, a cellar full of empty mailbags, and groceries for a battalion.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: And the board.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: And the Monopoly board. And a ketchup bottle. And a saucer somebody had put down on the floor, filled with milk, for the farm cat.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: No. The cat's saucer?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: The fingerprint men worked that house for days, and it was the homely little objects that talked. The board, the bottle, the saucer, a beer mug, the bathroom. Biggs's prints on the Monopoly set and the ketchup bottle. Others all over the vehicles and the cellar. One by one, the kitchen testified.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Last episode a sandwich, this week a whole pantry. There's a cookbook in these shows somewhere.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: And now the man doing the hunting. Detective Chief Superintendent Tommy Butler of the Flying Squad. The press called him the Grey Fox. Never married, lived with his mother, worked eighteen hour days and told nobody anything, and knew London's thieves the way Cordrey knew signals. Scotland Yard handed him a hand-picked squad and told him to bring in the train gang.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: And he's holding a farmhouse full of fingerprints belonging to men who are all, suddenly, very hard to find.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Some harder than others. Roger Cordrey, our signal florist, falls first, and it isn't fingerprints that get him. He's in Bournemouth with a friend and a car full of his share, and he rents a lock-up garage from a nice widow, paying three months in advance, in cash, on the spot.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Cash up front, no questions. Bold. Widows notice things.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: This widow especially, because she was the widow of a police officer. She rang the local station, and in the garage and the car the police found a hundred and forty one thousand pounds.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: [laughs] Of all the garages in England, he picked the police widow's.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: A week or so later, a couple out walking in the woods near Dorking come across a briefcase. Then a holdall. Then more. A hundred thousand pounds, sitting under the trees in the Surrey countryside. And tucked in among the notes, a receipt from a hotel in Germany, made out to a Herr and Frau Field.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Field. The farm lawyer. The paperwork guy!

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Brian Field, the clerk who arranged the purchase of Leatherslade Farm. His name in the money, his name on the conveyance, his firm in the files. The paperwork you warned about, Ruby, doing exactly what paperwork does.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: I said it! I said you don't buy the getaway house!

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Through the autumn, Butler's squad reels them in. Charlie Wilson, lifted at home in Clapham. Biggs, at home in Redhill, his prints still sitting on that ketchup bottle. Gordon Goody, who very nearly rode it out, until paint from the farm turned up on the soles of his shoes. Wisbey, Welch, Hussey. And Roy James, the racing driver, goes down like a racing driver: over the rooftops of St John's Wood with a holdall in his hand, caught at the end of a chase.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: And Reynolds? Napoleon himself?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Gone. Vanished, with his wife and baby son. So has Buster Edwards. So has Jimmy White. Put them in your pocket for the end of the show. But by January 1964, the Crown has a dozen men in the dock, and it stages the biggest criminal trial England had ever mounted.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Where do you even put a trial that size?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Aylesbury, the county town. Nothing there was big enough, so they converted the council offices into a courtroom. It ran fifty one days. The jury was out for more than sixty hours. And when the verdicts came back guilty, Mr Justice Edmund Davies looked down at the men in the dock and started handing out the sentences. Thirty years.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Thirty years. For a robbery?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Thirty years, seven men. Biggs, Goody, Wilson, Wisbey, Welch, Hussey, Roy James. And to put that number in its time: in 1964, men who had killed people were serving ten, twelve years. The judge said that showing these men leniency would be, his words, positively evil. Nothing like it had been handed down for theft in living memory.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: I keep going back and forth on it. They beat a man nearly to death, and I don't forget that. But thirty years, when killers got twelve? That's not a sentence, that's a message.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: That's exactly what it was, and the country argued about it then the way you're arguing now. And here is where the message curdles. Bill Boal.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Who is Bill Boal?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: A precision engineer. A friend of Cordrey's, who helped him hide money after the robbery, and was arrested beside him in Bournemouth. He was never at the track. Nearly everyone who has studied this case, including police officers in later years, came to believe he had nothing to do with the robbery at all. He was convicted anyway. Twenty four years, cut to fourteen on appeal. He died in prison in 1970.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: God. That one lands different.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Of everything in this story, that's the part the robbers themselves stayed angriest about, for whatever their word is worth. The one man most of them swore was innocent died inside, while half the guilty lived long enough to write their memoirs.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: And you said the verdicts were nearly across the board. Nearly. Who walked?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: John Daly, Reynolds' brother in law. He had been at the farm. His fingerprints were on the Monopoly board. But only on the Monopoly board. And his barrister stood up and made a beautifully simple argument: a board game is a movable object. You can play Monopoly anywhere. Nothing proves this game was played at that farm. The judge agreed, the case collapsed, and Daly walked out of that courtroom a free man.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Hang on. The board convicts Biggs and acquits Daly? The same board?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: The law is a strange machine. Prints on a house put you in the house. Prints on a game only put you at a game, and a game can be anywhere.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Somewhere out there a barrister dined out on that for forty years.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Now. You would think thirty year sentences close the story. Instead they light the fuse on its strangest chapter. Because within eighteen months, two of the seven are gone.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Gone as in...

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Escaped. August 1964, Winson Green prison, Birmingham. A crew breaks into the prison at three in the morning, overpowers a patrolling officer, opens Charlie Wilson's cell with copied keys, and walks him out the way they came. The whole operation takes about three minutes. At morning roll call, the silent man is simply not there.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: They broke INTO the prison. A jailbreak in reverse. That's showing off.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: It had never been done. Wilson eventually surfaces in a quiet suburb near Montreal, living as a mild Canadian family man, right up until January 1968, when there's a knock at the door and it's Tommy Butler, personally, four thousand miles from home. The family's calls back to England had been watched. Walls couldn't hold Charlie Wilson, but homesickness could.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: And escape number two?

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: July 1965, Wandsworth prison, London. Exercise yard. At a signal, a furniture van pulls up against the outside wall, a platform rises out of its roof, and a rope ladder drops into the yard. Ronnie Biggs goes up the ladder, down through the van, into a waiting car, and off the board entirely. Of his thirty years, he had served about fifteen months.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Biggs is the name everybody knows. And this is why, isn't it. Not the robbery. The running.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: The running, and where he ran to. But that belongs to the ending, and we owe some people their endings first.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Endings, then. Jimmy White, three years a ghost, caught quietly in Kent in 1966. Buster Edwards, who had run to Mexico, ran out of money and nerve, flew home the same year and gave himself up. Nine years inside. He spent the rest of his life selling flowers from a stall outside Waterloo Station.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: From a fifty first of two point six million to carnations on the pavement.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Bruce Reynolds lasted longest. Mexico, Canada, then home to England with the money gone, moving town to town. In November 1968, Tommy Butler opened one more door, in Torquay, and said, hello, Bruce. And Reynolds, dressing gown and all, said, c'est la vie. He served ten years, and the book he wrote afterwards is in our show notes.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: And Biggs. Finish Biggs.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Plastic surgery in Paris. A few quiet years in Australia, always one step ahead. And then Rio de Janeiro. And in Rio, in 1974, the law finally, literally, puts its hand on his shoulder. A Scotland Yard detective flies out to bring him home. But Biggs's Brazilian girlfriend is expecting his child, and Brazil does not extradite the father of a Brazilian child. So the detective flies home with an empty seat, and Biggs stays. Untouchable, and broke, so he starts selling the only thing he has left: being Ronnie Biggs. T-shirts, barbecues, photos with tourists, tell your friends you met the train robber. Thirty six years of it. In 2001, old and sick, he finally flew home voluntarily, into custody. Released in 2009. He died in 2013.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: He turned getting caught into the longest con of the lot.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: And the money. Of two point six million pounds, the police recovered less than four hundred thousand. Cordrey's garages, the woods at Dorking, and one night in December 1963, a phone box in south London with forty seven thousand pounds sitting inside it, left anonymously. Somebody's nerve, giving out. The rest went to minders, fixers, lawyers, bad investments, and thin air. Most of the gang walked out of prison years later to find the people minding their shares had minded them down to nothing.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: So almost nobody kept it. The money just... evaporated.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: And two more accounts to settle, because this show keeps its books. Jack Mills went back to light duties, but he was never the same man, and the compensation he received came to two hundred and fifty pounds. He died in 1970. Officially of leukemia, unconnected to that night. His family always said the cosh started the decline. The record disagrees with itself, so you get both versions, and you can sit with them like the rest of us. His fireman, David Whitby, died of a heart attack at thirty four.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Two hundred and fifty pounds. Out of two point six million. That's the number from this one I'm keeping.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: And the Ulsterman, the inside man, the one who was never caught. Fifty years on, Gordon Goody, by then an old man running a bar in Spain, finally gave the world a name: Patrick McKenna, a Belfast-born postal worker living in Salford. If it's true, McKenna never spent a visible penny of his share and died with his family knowing nothing at all. But it was one old thief telling one last story, and plenty of people who know the case don't buy it. Accounts differ. They may always differ.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: So the only one who fully got away with it might be a man who never touched the money again. There's something almost holy about that.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Next time on Two Grifters, I pay my debts. Paris, 1925. A dying newspaper story, a fake government office, a room full of scrap metal dealers, and the smoothest man in Europe. The man who sold the Eiffel Tower. Twice.

transcript.speaker: Ruby
transcript.text: Finally! Until then: mind the doors, be kind to your train driver, and never, ever play Monopoly at the safe house.

transcript.speaker: Frankie
transcript.text: Two Grifters is an independent show. When the record disagrees with itself, we say so on air, and the sources for every episode are in the show notes.
